coredata.dc:title: Stress-dissipated conductive polymer binders for high-stability silicon anode in lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of The Chinese Ceramic Society.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of The Chinese Ceramic Society.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhong
Chu, Xiang
Wang, Keli
Zhang, Haitao
He, Zhongqian
Xie, Yanting
Yang, Weiqing
coredata.dc:description: Silicon-based anodes with high theoretical capacity have intriguing potential applications for high energy density lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), while suffer from immense volumetric change and brittle solid-state electrolyte interface that causes collapse of electrodes. Here, a stress-dissipated conductive polymer binder (polyaniline with citric acid, PC) is developed to enhance the mechanical electrochemical performance between Si nanoparticles (SiNPs) and binders. Benefiting from the stable triangle network node of citric acid and a considerable distributed of hydroxyl groups, the PC binder can effectively dissipate the stress from SiNPs, thus providing an excellent cyclic stability of Si anodes. Both experimental results and theoretical calculation demonstrate the enhanced adhesion between binders and SiNPs could bond the particles tightly to form a robust electrode. The as-fabricated Si anode exhibits outstanding structural stability upon long-term cycles that exhibit a highly reversible capability of 1 021 mA·h·g−1 over 500 cycles at a current density of 0.5 C (1 C = 4 200 mA·g−1). Evidently, this stress-dissipated binder design will provide a promising route to achieve long-life Si-based LIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Conductive polymer
Silicon anode
Binder
Mechanical electrochemistry
Lithium-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed mechano-electrochemical stress dissipation mechanism of PANi@CA conductive polymer binder for Silicon anode. (a) Schematic ilustration of the pulverization of the SiNPs electrodes with commercial binder during cycling. (b) Schematic illustration of the operation of PANi@CA binder to dissipate the stress during repeated volume changes of SiNPs. (c) Chemical structures of 3D networks structred PANi@CA conductive polymer binder.

Schematically illustrating the fabrication of PANi@CA electrode and its morphologies and microstructure. (a) Precursor solution A and B of PANi@CA. (b) 3D PANi@CA solution after in-situ polymerization. (c) PANi@CA@SiNP prepared from spray-coating method. (d) pattern can be easily obtained through spraying conductive polymer ink onto substrates. (e) TEM image of PANi@CA@SiNP. (f) SEM image of PANi@CA nanosheet and (g) corresponding EDS mapping results, (h) XRD and (i) FTIR spectrums of PANi@CA. (j) Electronic conductivity of PANi@CA.

Adhesion property of PANi@CA binder. (a) Schematic diagram of mechanical stripping test and (b) corresponding results of differernt binders. (c) AFM image of Si-PC5 electrode. (d) Young's modulus of Si-PC5 electrode tested by AFM. (e) Theoretical calculation of Si-PC5 structures and (f) adsorption energy of with/without Si.

Electrochemical performances of the Si electrodes with different PANi@CA binders. (a) Cyclic voltammetry curves of the Si-PC5 electrodes at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. (b) Cyclic voltammetry curves of the Si-PC5 electrodes at different scan rate. (c) Electrochemical stable window of the Si-PC5. (d) The EIS plots of SiNP electrodes with different binders. (e) Rate capabilites of the different Si-PC electrodes at different current densities. (f) The 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th and 5th galvanostatic discharge-charge profiles of the Si-PC5 electrodes in a potential range of 0.01–1.20 V versus Li+/Li. (g) Cycling performance and Coulombic efficiency of the different Si-PC electrodes at 0.5C up to 500 cycles. The Si mass loading is ∼1.5 mg cm−2.

Cyclic stabilization mechanism of the Si electrodes with PC binders. (a) Pristine cross-sectional SEM image of Si-PC5 electrode. (b) cross-sectional and (c) top SEM images of Si-PC5 electrodes after 500 cycles. (d) Schematic illustration of cyclic stabilization mechanism of the SiNPs with PC binder. (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s, and (g) F 1s results of pristine and 500th cycling electrodes.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of The Chinese Ceramic Society.

head.ce:title: Stress-dissipated conductive polymer binders for high-stability silicon anode in lithium-ion batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education)
School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhong Xu, Xiang Chu and Keli Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon-based anodes with high theoretical capacity have intriguing potential applications for high energy density lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), while suffer from immense volumetric change and brittle solid-state electrolyte interface that causes collapse of electrodes. Here, a stress-dissipated conductive polymer binder (polyaniline with citric acid, PC) is developed to enhance the mechanical electrochemical performance between Si nanoparticles (SiNPs) and binders. Benefiting from the stable triangle network node of citric acid and a considerable distributed of hydroxyl groups, the PC binder can effectively dissipate the stress from SiNPs, thus providing an excellent cyclic stability of Si anodes. Both experimental results and theoretical calculation demonstrate the enhanced adhesion between binders and SiNPs could bond the particles tightly to form a robust electrode. The as-fabricated Si anode exhibits outstanding structural stability upon long-term cycles that exhibit a highly reversible capability of 1 021 mA·h·g−1 over 500 cycles at a current density of 0.5 C (1 C = 4 200 mA·g−1). Evidently, this stress-dissipated binder design will provide a promising route to achieve long-life Si-based LIBs.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon (Si) anodes have been received much attention for the next-generation lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) anodes due to their high specific capacity (4 200 mA·h·g−1) [1]. However, the severe volume expansion effect of Si (∼300%) during lithiation/delithiation process as well as their inferior electric conductivity would jointly cause the collapse of the fragile conductive network, leading to rapid capacity decay [2,3]. The conventional binders of polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) for LIBs are inappropriate for the Si electrode application, due to their weak van der Waals interactions between Si and binders, resulting in pulverization of Si nanoparticles (SiNPs) and the forfeit of electronic connection during lithiation/delithiation process (Fig. 1 a) [4,5].

Thus, several advanced binders (e.g., carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC-Na), poly (acrylic acid), alginate [6–8]) with higher Young's module and abundant functional groups have been reported to improve the mechanical stability of SiNPs electrodes via utilizing the native linear/3D skeleton and polar functional groups [9–12]. However, these three-component electrode consisting conductive additive and binder would severely decrease the volumetric and gravimetric capacity of the electrode. In comparison with three-component electrode, two-component electrode based on conductive polymeric binder could effectively increase the volumetric capacity of Si anodes via replacing inactive conductive additives [13,14]. Nevertheless, these linear polymers with brittle interchain interactions are still not stable enough to ensure the structural stability of the Si anodes during cycling [15,16]. Therefore, it is necessary to design three-dimensional (3D) conductive binders with superior mechanical properties for Si anodes [17].

Herein, we report a stress-dissipated 3D conductive polymer binder strategy for Si anodes in which polyaniline (PANi) are in-situ polymerized with citric acid (CA) via hydrogen bond. The polymeric binder shows excellent mechanical stability due to its extremely simple structural design. Three carboxyl groups on the end of CA chain are regarded as the three vertices of triangle, which could considerably enhance adhesion force and commendably dissipate stress from the SiNPs expansion through the self-healing hydrogen bonds (Fig. 1b-c). This stress-dissipated strategy could ensure constant electrical connections even as the Si particles disintegrate into pieces during lithiation/delithiation. Resulted, this unique PANi@CA (PC) conductive polymeric binder could endure dramatic volume expansion of SiNPs to achieve high capacity (2 598 mA·h·g−1 at 0.1 C) and excellent cycle life (1021 mA h g−1 after 500 cycles). Therefore, this stress-dissipated strategy will greatly promote the rapid development of high-capacity electrode.

Aniline and citric acid are purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) (∼50 nm), conductive carbon of acetylene black, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), polyacrylic acid (PAA) binders and N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent are obtained from Kejing Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrogen chloride (HCl) (37.5%) and ammonium persulfate (APS) are purchased from Chengdu Kelon. Except aniline is vacuum distilled before use, other reagents are direct used as received without further purification.

To attain conductive polymeric binder (PANi@CA, PC), PC with different aniline to citric acid ratio were prepared as report in our previously work [18]. Specifically, 8.56 g APS is dissolved in 30 mL deionized (DI) water to form solution A. 2.63 g citric acid monohydrate, 5.75 mL aniline (with a molar ratio of 5:1 to citric acid) and 3.4 mL 6 mol/L HCl solution are added into 15 mL DI water to form solution B. Then the mixed A and B solution is kept cooled at 0 °C and stirred with glass rod quickly. The mixed solution is kept at ice bath for 12 h for complete polymerization and crosslinking. The acquired PANi@CA hydrogel is then rinsed with excess DI water through vacuum filtration to remove excess impurities and by-products. Finally, the filter cake is transfer to vacuum drying oven to remove excess solvents to obtain conductive polymer binders. For concise illustration, the different ratios (the ratio of aniline to citric acid is 1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 1:7, 1:9) are presented as PC1, PC3, PC5, PC7, PC9, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silicon anode with conductive polymer binder
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2 g PC binder and 0.96 g SiNPs powder are added to 200 mL DI water to obtain Si-PC solution, and the SiNPs are uniform dispersed in solution by using a sonicator for 30 min. Then the prepared Si-PC anodes are uniformly sprayed on the copper foil by means of high-pressure spraying. In order to conduct a comparative test, apart from preparing the Si-PC anode, we also prepared the Si anodes with commercial binder (CMC, PVDF and PAA) and applied them to Si-based LIBs for comparison.

Morphologies and microstructure of the PC binder and the pristine/cycled Si-PC electrodes are examined by SEM, TEM, Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The electrical conductivity of PC binder is measured using a four-point probe technique. The mechanical properties of the electrodes are measured on a LDW1 Tensile Tester (100 N Load Cell). The Young's modulus of PC binder is tested by atomic force microscope (AFM).

The electrodes are cut into 12 mm diameter disk to assemble the CR2032-type coin cells with lithium foil (Tianjin Zhongneng Lithium Industry Co., Ltd, Diameter is 15.6 mm) as the counter electrode and polypropylene (Celgard 2400) as the separator. The electrolyte consisted of 1 mol/L LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/diethyl carbonate (1:1 by volume) solution with 10% (in mass) fluoroethylene carbonates (FEC). The entire battery assembling process is conducted in Argon glove box (H2O and O2 is < 0.1 × 10-6, in volume).

The electrochemical properties of the samples are studied using CR2032-type coin cells. The coin cells are tested from 0.01 V to 1.2 V versus Li+/Li at 25 °C using a computer-controlled battery test system (NEWARE CT2100, Shenzhen, China) at the current density of 0.1 C, 0.2 C, 0.5 C, 1 C and 2 C (1 C = 4 200 mA h g−1). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements are carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, China) with different scan rate in the voltage range of 0.01–1.20 V. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the symmetric and full cells are conducted on the CHI660E in the range of 0.1 Hz–1.0 MHz with a potential amplitude of 10 mV.

All the spin-polarized calculations are performed using the Gaussian 16, which is plane-wave density functional code. Based on the optimized structure, the absorption energy of the respective solvent molecules is calculated. The absorption energy can be calculated by the following equation: E absorption = E Si-PC-E PC -E Sibulk .

The synthesis process of conductive polymer (PANi@CA, PC) is schematically illustrated in Fig. 2 a. Typically, the aniline and citric acid (CA) are mixed as precursor solution A according to an optimized molar ratio of 1:5. And the oxidative initiator ammonium persulfate (APS) as solution B is added into solution A to trigger the polymerization and cross-linking of aniline with CA. The precursor could spontaneously interconnect to form 3D PC hydrogel through the in-situ polymerization process. As shown in Fig. 2b, the color of mixed solution changed from transparent to dark green, which indicates the formation of polyaniline in the state of emeraldine salt after the polymerization reaction [19]. In addition, the polymerized PC binder would lose fluid properties and turn into hydrogel in about 15 min, suggesting the CA molecules are efficiently grafted onto the PANi backbone chains. Due to the supramolecular interactions between PANi and CA. The PC nanosheets could be broken through vigorous stirring or ultrasonication. Thus, the SiNPs can easily dispersed into these nanosheets in water to form Si-PC ink. This ink can be uniformly sprayed onto a copper collector (Fig. 2c and Fig. S1) and a variety of patterns on other substrates to produce Si-PC electrodes (Fig. 2d).

The morphology of PC binders is demonstrated by TEM and SEM characterizations. As shown in Fig. 2e and Fig. S2, several SiNPs connected with 3D network structure can be observed in the TEM and SEM images, indicating considerable adhesion and dispersion of PC binders. SEM image shows the massive layered structure with approximate 3 μm width of PC nanosheets (Fig. 2f). And the corresponding EDS mapping shows the PANi and CA are homogeneously dispersed in the nanosheets (Fig. 2g and Fig. S3). Fig. 2h presents the XRD patterns of different PC binders, showing two typical peaks at 19.8° and 24.5° which could be assigned to (0 2 0) and (2 0 0) reflections of PANi, respectively. And the peak of 5.9° can be considered of CA. The gradually increased peak intensity and full width at half maximum (FWHM) indicates that the higher PC ratio of binder possesses a partially ordered structure [20,21]. Meanwhile, the XRD patterns of Si electrode with other commercial binders are shown in Figs. S4 and S5. The peaks of Si match perfectly with JCD-PDF#01–0787. And other commercial binders also show their own specific peaks.

The interaction between PANi and CA molecules is investigated through FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 2i, the peak at 3 420 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibrations of NH units on the benzene ring, while the broad peak at 3 070 cm−1 is related to stretching vibration of end NH units. The oxidation state of PC binder can be identified by the peaks around 1 500 cm−1, which are associated with the aromatic ring stretching [20]. Meanwhile, the strong peaks around 850 cm−1 could be described as the degree of delocalization of electrons and thus it is the characteristic peak for the conductivity of PC binder [22]. Therefore, the PC5 with strong peak intensity exhibits the highest electronic conductivity of 5 S cm−1 (Fig. 2j).

The mechanical properties is a key parameter of binder for commercial application. Binders with robust mechanical properties could effectively tolerate the huge volume expansion of the SiNPs, maintaining the structural stability of the electrodes, and thus provide reliable performances of Si-based LIBs [15,23]. To evaluate the mechanical properties of the PC binders, mechanical stripping tests of different PC binders are conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the SiNPs electrode materials would be gradually removed from the copper foil by the tape through a Tensile Tester. The corresponding peel test results are shown in Fig. 3b and S6. The PC5 binder exhibits the highest average peel strength (∼0.75 N) than other commercial binders. The high adhesive property of the PC5 is mainly due to the high hydroxyl content and the resulting large number of hydrogen bonds. Young's modulus of PC5 binders have been characterized through the AFM. It is reported that the AFM probe can simulates the stress caused by the volume change from the SiNPs [24,25]. In Fig. 3c and d, the average Young's modulus of the Si-PC5 electrodes is about 35.85 GPa, suggesting the high adhesion of the PC5 binder and excellent mechanical strength of the electrode [26]. In order to further explore the adsorption property of PC5 binder for SiNPs, density functional theory (DFT) calculation has been conducted. The structure before and after adsorption SiNPs and the corresponding absorption energy are shown in Fig. 3e-f. The absorption energy of the PC5 binder for SiNPs is approximate −0.88 Hartree, which indicates PC5 binder could effectively adsorb SiNPs.

Since the PC5 binders possess the higher adhesion and mechanical strength than the commercial binders. We further explore the electrochemical performance of Si-PC5 electrode for LIBs. Fig. 4 displays the cycle performance of SiNPs electrodes with different binders. Fig. 4a shows the CV curves of SiNP anodes with PC5 binders, an apparent peak at 0.6 V can be regarded as the formation of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer on the surface of SiNPs during the initial scan with PC5 binder [27,28]. The sharp peak appears at ∼0.04 V represents the cystallization process, where the amorphous Li x Si phase rapidly transform into the cystalline Li15Si4 at the end of the discharge process [29,30]. From the second cycle, a reduction peak at 0.17 V could be clearly observed and it involves the delithiation process that, the amorphous Li x Si gradually convert into prinstine SiNPs. Moreover, the oxidation peak at 0.46 V could be considered as a reversible electrochemical process, where Li15Si4 phase generated at the end of cathodic process would preferetially transform into the Li x Si phase and finally to the amorphous SiNP phase [31,32]. Meanwhile, the area of CV curves that increases with the number of cycling, could be ascribed to the gradual activation of the Si-PC5 anodes. In Fig. 4b and S7, the CV measurement of different binders with different scan rates are also carried out. With the increase of scan rate, the peak shifts to higher voltage and the polarization phonomenon becomes more severe in all anodes. However, the curves overlap decently at higher scan rate on both peak location and intensity, suggesting the superior stability and reversiblity of Si-PC5 anode [33]. Fig. 4c shows the electrochemical stable performance of pure PC5 binder, the very weak oxidation-reduction current suggests that PC5 binder could maintain excellent electrochemical stability between 0.01 and 3.00 V. Therefore, the capacity of the binders is negligible compared to the whole electrode active materials. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of electrodes with different binder before cycling is shown in Fig. 4d. These curves are consists of a semicircle in the medium-high frequency region and a sloping line in the low frequency region, representing for the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and Warburg resistance, respectively [34]. It is obvious that the Si-PC5 anode exhibits a smallest R ct (∼79 Ω) than that of other commercial binders. The smaller resistance of Si-PC5 anode could be ascribed to the robust contact between the PC5 binder and SiNPs. Specifically, the CA molecules play an important part in bonding the SiNPs and PANi conductive network. The PC5 binder succeeded in changing the type of electronic contact from point contact to face contact, accelerating the transport of ion and electrons.

The rate capabilities of SiNP anode with different binders (Fig. 4e and Fig. S8) are conducted under the current density increasing from 0.1 C to 1.0 C (1 C = 4 200 mA g−1). For the Si-PC5 anodes, as the current increases stepwise from 0.1 to 0.2, 0.5 and 1.0C, the cell can deliver reversible specific capacities of 2 598, 2 236, 1 884 and 1 301 mA h g−1, respectively. More importantly, after multiple cycled under high current density, a reversible specific capacity of 2 735 mA h g−1 can still be recovered as the current density recover to the 0.1 C. When compared with other commercial binders, the rate performance of the Si-PC5 anode is quite steady and satisfactory.

In addition to the rate performance, the long-term cycling performance of Si anode is critical for the practical application. The galvanostatic charge/discharge at the current density of 0.5 C are shown in Fig. 4g and S9. The specific capacity is decreased at the initial cycles and then increased after a few cycles, which is due to the electrode polarizaiton at the high current density. It can be seen that even after 500 cycles at the current density of 0.5 C, the reversible specific capacities of 1 021 mA h g−1 can be retained. These results demonstrate that this PC5 binder from stress-dissipated conductive binder strategy could promote the excellent Li+ ion and electron transfer capability and highly mechanical stability of the Si anode [35–37].

To better understand the physicochemical effect of PC5 binder on the Si anode, the overall morphology of the electrodes after 500 charge-discharge cycles with different binders are characterized by SEM and XPS methods and the results are displayed in Fig. 5 and S10. For the Si-PC5 anode, a crack-free and smooth surface could be clearly observed after 500 charge/discharge cycles in Fig. 5 a-c. The thickness of the cycled Si anode is increased only by 6.99 μm, which indicates the excellent mechanical stability of PC5 binders could effectively promote the adhesion of SiNPs with the conductive network, thus leading to long-cycle stability [38,39]. The EIS curves of half-batteries are performed before and after cycles (Fig. S11). The Si-PC5 reveals a significant decrease in the resistance, which suggests the formation of a stable SEI layer during the cycling process (Fig. 5d). In this regard, the PC5 binder plays a vital role in retaining a stable conductive network and SEI layer for Si anode. The superior performance of Si-PC5 electrodes could be attributed to the unique properties of PC5 binder including the stress-dissipated triangular structure and enhanced supermolecular interaction with SiNPs [40].

The XPS are conducted to investage the interactions among the different polymer chains and SiNPs. As shwon in Fig. 5e-g, the C 1s spectra of Si-PC5 electrode at ∼288 eV represents the signal of −LiCOO, while the Li2CO3 signal appears in O 1s spectra ∼532 eV at after 500 cycles. This phenomenon indicates that the SEI layer are formed and part of them undergo phase transition from −LiCOO to Li2CO3. In addition, a Li x PF y /Li x PO y F z signal at ∼688 eV and a LiF signal at 683.8 eV in the F 1s spectrum after 500 cycling could be clearly observed. These results demonstrate that the SEI flim with organic (Li x PF y /Li x PO y F z ) and inorganic (LiF) coexisting could be formed more quickly on the surface of Si-PC5 electrode [41–43].It is reported that the inorganic layer is more rigid than organic layer. Therefore, the inorganic SEI layer is much easier to be broken by the volume expansion of SiNPs, leading to the revealing of fresh Si surface, continuously electrolyte consuming and affecting the battery life. This composite organic-inorganic SEI layer produced by Si-PC5 electrodes is more compatible with the volume change of the SiNPs. Moreover, this rigid-soft SEI layer can effectively work together with PC5 binder to relive the assault from repeat lithiation/delithiation and avoid the further direct contact between electrolyte and SiNPs to improve the coulombic efficiency of the battery [31,44,45].

In summary, a stress-dissipated conductive binder strategy with facile synthesis method and ultra-efficient binder performance is demonstrated, which enables a slurry-spraying electrode with high mechanical strength and long cycle life (500 cycles). this stress-dissipated conductive binder strategy enables the Si electrode with retention capability of 1 021 mA h g−1 after 500 continuously deep cycles at the current density of 0.5 C. The excellent performance of the Si-PC5 electrode is mainly attributed to the conductive network and stress-dissipated triangular structure of PC5 binder. Physical cross-linking of the conductive network and efficient interactions between PC5 binder and SiNPs revealed by experimental analysis and theoretical calculation indicate the formation of stable SEI layer and a mechanically robust conductive network. Considering the simple synthesis method and excellent binder performance, the developed PC5 binder is expected to achieve high energy density and long cycle life Si anode for next-generation energy storage devices.

Conceptualization, Z. X and H. T and W. Y.; Methodology, Z. X and Z. H.; Formal Analysis, X. C, Z, H.; Investigation, Z. X, X. C and Y, X.; Resources, Q. W, Y. X.; Writing-Original Draft, Z. X and H. T.; Writing-Review & Editing, Z. X, H. T and W. Y.; Funding Acquisition, H. T and W. Y. All the authors commented on and revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University and Key Laboratory of Neutron Physics for providing testing services. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51977185, 51972277), Southwest Jiaotong University Science and Technology Rising Star Program (No. 2682021CG021).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmat.2022.09.013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alloy negative electrodes for Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.N. Obrovac, V.L. Chevrier, Alloy negative electrodes for Li-ion batteries, Chem. Rev. 114(23) (2014) 11444-11502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance lithium battery anodes using silicon nanowires

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.K. Chan, H. Peng, G. Liu, K. McIlwrath, X.F. Zhang, R.A. Huggins, Y. Cui, High-performance lithium battery anodes using silicon nanowires, Nat. Nanotechnol. 3(1) (2008) 31-35.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in silicon-based electrodes: from fundamental research toward practical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ge, C. Cao, G.M. Biesold, C.D. Sewell, S.M. Hao, J. Huang, W. Zhang, Y. Lai, Z. Lin, Recent Advances in Silicon-Based Electrodes: From Fundamental Research toward Practical Applications, Adv. Mater. 33(16) (2021) 2004577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of the design of advanced binders for high-performance batteries

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design criteria for silicon-based anode binders in half and full cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Deng, Y. Zheng, X. Zheng, T. Or, Q. Ma, L. Qian, Y. Deng, A. Yu, J. Li, Z. Chen, Design Criteria for Silicon-Based Anode Binders in Half and Full Cells, Adv. Energy Mater. (2022) 2200850.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stress-relieving defects enable ultra-stable silicon anode for Li-ion storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhou, Y. Yang, G. Hou, D. Yi, B. Zhou, S. Chen, T.D. Lam, F. Yuan, D. Golberg, X. Wang, Stress-relieving defects enable ultra-stable silicon anode for Li-ion storage, Nano Energy 70 (2020) 104568.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhong Xu received his B.S. from Southwest Jiaotong University in 2014. He is currently pursuing Ph.D. degree in materials science and engineering at Southwest Jiaotong University.
Xiang Chu received his B.E. from Southwest Jiaotong University in 2018. He is currently pursuing Ph.D. degree in materials science and engineering at Southwest Jiaotong University.
Keli Wang received his B.E. from Sichuan University in 2022. He is currently pursuing Ph.D. degree in materials science and engineering at Sichuan University.

Dr Haitao Zhang Distinguished Researcher, Ph.D., received his B.S. and M.S. degrees in Materials Science and Engineering from Hainan University from 2008 to 2015; and his Ph.D. degree in Electrical Engineering from the Institute of Electrical Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2015. He is a recipient of 100 excellent PhD theses from Chinese Academy of Sciences (2016), and was selected as the Eagle Scholar in Southwest Jiaotong University in 2017. He has published more than 120 SCI papers in top international journals such as Nano Today, ACS Nano, Nano Energy, etc.

Dr Weiqing Yang is currently a full professor at the Southwest Jiaotong University. He received his MS in Physics (2007), and PhD in Materials Science and Engineering (2011) from Sichuan University. He was a post-doctorate research fellow at Georgia Institute of Technology from 2013 to 2014, under the supervision of Prof. Zhong Lin Wang. He has published more than 200 papers. Currently, his main research interest includes energy harvesting and storage devices.
